coredata.dc:title: A sandwich-like PtCo-graphene/carbon dots/graphene catalyst for efficient methanol oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Shaohong
Zhang, Feifei
Gao, Cuili
Xia, Jianfei
Lu, Lin
Wang, Zonghua
coredata.dc:description: The PtCo-graphene/carbon dots/graphene (PtCo/GCG) electrocatalyst was prepared on a glassy carbon electrode via electrodeposition of PtCo nanoparticles onto the sandwich-structured graphene/carbon dots/graphene (GCG) composite. The obtained PtCo/GCG electrocatalyst has been characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, scanning electron microscopy and also electrochemical measurements. Structure characterization indicates that the PtCo/GCG catalyst has good layered graphene morphology, with PtCo nanoparticles uniformly dispersed between the graphene layers. Electrochemical results indicate that PtCo/GCG catalyst has much better electrocatalytic activity and stability toward methanol oxidation when compared with PtCo/graphene and Pt catalysts. The improved performance is mainly owing to the embedding of carbon dots into the graphene layers, and the synergistic effect between Pt and Co nanoparticles. Carbon dots effectively adsorbed onto graphene layers, which increase the electrical conductivity, enhance the dispersion of PtCo nanoparticles, and subsequently enlarge the reactive surface area with more active sites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PtCo nanocatalyst
Carbon dots
Graphene
Electrodeposition
Methanol oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Estimation of electrochemical activity surface area (ECSA)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the prepared carbon dots, (B) Fluorescence intensity of carbon dots solution excited under different excitation wavelength. Insert is the optical photographs of carbon dots solution placed under daylight lamp (a), and irradiated by 350nm UV light (b).
SEM images of (a) PtCo/GCG and (c) PtCo/GN and large scale (b) PtCo/GCG and (d) PtCo/GN. TEM image of (e) PtCo/GCG catalyst and XRD patterns of (f) PtCo/GCG and Pt/GCG.
CVs of (a) Pt, (b) PtCo/GN, and (c) PtCo/GCG in 0.5M H2SO4 solution within a potential range of −0.3 to 1.2V at 50mVs−1.
CVs of (a) Pt/GCE, (b) PtCo/GN/GCE, and (c) PtCo/GCG/GCE in the mixed aqueous solution consisting of 0.5M H2SO4 and 0.5M methanol within a potential range of 0 to 1.2V at a rate of 50mVs−1.
Chronoamperometric curves of (a) Pt/GCE, (b) PtCo/GN/GCE, and (c) PtCo/GCG/GCE in the mixed aqueous solution containing 0.5M H2SO4 and 0.5M methanol at 0.6V for 3600s.
Synthetic steps of the sandwich-like PtCo/GCG catalyst.
Comparison of the synthesized catalysts with some other related reports on electrocatalytic activity toward methanol oxidation.

head.ce:title: A sandwich-like PtCo-graphene/carbon dots/graphene catalyst for efficient methanol oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textile, The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory, Shandong Sino-Japanese Center for Collaborative Research of Carbon Nanomaterials, Collaborative Innovation Center for Marine Biomass Fiber Materials and Textiles, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textile
The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zibo Normal College, Zibo, Shandong 255200, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The PtCo-graphene/carbon dots/graphene (PtCo/GCG) electrocatalyst was prepared on a glassy carbon electrode via electrodeposition of PtCo nanoparticles onto the sandwich-structured graphene/carbon dots/graphene (GCG) composite. The obtained PtCo/GCG electrocatalyst has been characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, scanning electron microscopy and also electrochemical measurements. Structure characterization indicates that the PtCo/GCG catalyst has good layered graphene morphology, with PtCo nanoparticles uniformly dispersed between the graphene layers. Electrochemical results indicate that PtCo/GCG catalyst has much better electrocatalytic activity and stability toward methanol oxidation when compared with PtCo/graphene and Pt catalysts. The improved performance is mainly owing to the embedding of carbon dots into the graphene layers, and the synergistic effect between Pt and Co nanoparticles. Carbon dots effectively adsorbed onto graphene layers, which increase the electrical conductivity, enhance the dispersion of PtCo nanoparticles, and subsequently enlarge the reactive surface area with more active sites.

ce:section.ce:para: With the worldwide increasing energy demand and the escalating environmental problems, it is urgent to develop alternative energy sources which are much more environment-friendly. Among different kinds of sustainable energy systems, the direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) is one of the most promising energy generators owing to its superior performances, such as high theoretical energy density, high energy conversion efficiency, low operating temperature, excellent environment-friendliness, and easy transportation and storage [1]. However, the commercial application of DMFC is still hindered by several factors, and one of the key problems is a lack of rational design of the anode catalysts, which can result in poor catalytic activity and easy deactivation of the catalysts [2].

Among all the transition metals, Pt possesses the highest activity for dissociative adsorption of methanol [3], but the kinetics for methanol oxidation is slow when pure Pt is used as anode catalyst and it is also easily poisoned by the intermediate carbonaceous products (such as CO) during methanol oxidation [4]. Moreover, in view of the high cost and low abundance of Pt, it is difficult for its wide commercial application as electrocatalyst [5]. One of the most effective methods to solve the above problems is to alloy Pt with less expensive metals including Fe, Co, Pd, Ni, Cu, Sn [6–13]. Pt-based bimetallic alloy electrocatalysts have attracted increasing attention because it can not only reduce the dosage of the noble metal, but also enhance its CO tolerance and electrocatalytic activity significantly [14]. The enhanced performance in these catalysts has been ascribed to several factors, for instance, the synergistic effect, d-band centre shift and lattice shrinking [15,16]. In comparison with many Pt-binary alloy catalysts, PtCo bimetallic systems are known to exhibit superior catalytic performance toward methanol oxidation [8,17], thus holding great promise as efficient anode catalysts in DMFCs.

For the purpose of further increasing the activity of noble metal-based catalysts and reducing the dosage of precious metals, it is of great necessity to load metal materials on the surface of supporting materials which have large surface area and high electrical conductivity [18]. Graphene (GN) has recently received increasing interest in a wide range of fields, owing to its excellent mechanical, thermal, electrical, optical properties and extremely large surface area [19,20]. In recent years, some research groups have investigated graphene as catalyst support. It has been acknowledged that the introduction of graphene into electrocatalyst system can significantly enhance the dispersion of Pt-based alloy nanoparticles, and can also effectively improve the electrocatalytic performance of Pt for methanol oxidation [21]. This is mainly owing to the outstanding properties of graphene: excellent electric conductivity and thermal conductivity, high surface area, strong mechanical strength, various surface functional groups and lattice defects serving as anchors to immobilize metal nanoparticles [22].

Despite above mentioned advantages, there still exist limitations when graphene is used as catalyst support. It is well known that there are π-π stacking interactions and Van der Waals force between graphene layers [23]. Therefore, the irreversible agglomeration of graphene will make it difficult for Pt-based nanoparticles anchoring uniformly on it. Recently, there has been an effective way to improve the performance of the catalysts by combing nanomaterials with graphene [24,25]. A sandwich-like graphene/carbon nanosphere/graphene composite was reported and used for half-cell oxygen reduction which showed good stability and high activity [26]. The Pt/CeO2/graphene nanocomposites were synthesized with remarkably improved electrocatalytic performances [27]. Although a lot of efforts have been made to prepare graphene-based catalyst supports, it is still essential to design an effective way for synthesizing graphene supported catalysts with outstanding electrocatalytic performance [23].

Herein, a facile method to synthesize the graphene/carbon dots/graphene composite supported PtCo electrocatalyst (PtCo/GCG) is presented. In this graphene-based composite, carbon dots are used as pillars between the graphene layers [28]. As is shown in Scheme 1 , the conductive carbon dots are anchored between graphene layers. To promote the electrocatalytic property of the anode catalyst in DMFC for methanol oxidation, we introduce carbon dots into the graphene layered structure. The advantages of introducing carbon dots between graphene layers could be described as follows: (1) the embedded carbon dots can broaden the space between different graphene layers, which is beneficial to the diffusion of reaction species; (2) the electron conductivity between different graphene nanosheets can be significantly enhanced by introducing the conductive carbon dots as “bridge” [29]; (3) the optimized two-dimensional graphene sheets by introducing carbon dots can improve the dispersion of PtCo nanoparticles, which will improve the utilization of Pt and the electrocatalytic activity of methanol oxidation; (4) carbon dots can provide functional groups (such as OH, COOH) which can facilitate the consumption of intermediate species [23].

The synthesis of the PtCo/GCG electrocatalyst mainly includes three steps. Firstly, carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Secondly, the carbon dots were deposited on the surface of graphene through ultrasonic treatment. Finally, the electrodeposition method was taken to load PtCo nanoparticles onto the GCG composite modified glassy carbon electrode. PtCo nanoparticles were embedded between two adjacent layers of graphene. The as-prepared PtCo/GCG electrocatalyst exhibited significant enhancement in electrocatalytic performance owing to its sandwich-like structure.

Co(NO3)2·6H2O, methanol, ethanol, and acetone were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. H2PtCl6·6H2O, β-cyclodextrin, and H2SO4 solution were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Natural graphite powder was provided by Qingdao Fujin graphite Co., Ltd., China. Graphene is prepared in considerable quantities from natural graphite by using a previously reported method [30], utilizing high-intensity cavitation field in a pressurized ultrasound reactor (YPM11B-MB, 20kHz, 3000W, Hangzhou Chenggong Ultrasonic Equip Co., Ltd., China). The chemical reagents used throughout the experiment were analytical grade. Doubly distilled water was used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

The morphology of the synthesized samples was characterized by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100) and a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6700F) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed with a DX2700 diffractometer (China) using Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5418Å). All electrochemical tests were conducted on a CHI 660C electrochemical workstation (Shanghai, China) with a three-electrode system. A platinum wire was used as the counter electrode; Saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and glassy carbon electrode (GCE) were used as the reference and working electrode, respectively. All potentials reported in this work were referred to SCE.

The cherry tomato was squeezed by a juicer at room temperature and 70ml of the obtained juice was transferred into a beaker. Subsequently, 2mg of β-cyclodextrin was added to the beaker with magnetically stirring for 10min. Then the above mixture was sealed in an autoclave, placed into a drying oven, heated to 180°C and allowed to remain here for 20h. After cooling to room temperature, the autoclave was taken out from the oven. The products were centrifuged (1000rpm) for 15min. Then, the obtained supernatant was filtered and subsequently poured into a rotary evaporator with temperature set at 40°C. Till only a little liquid left, 20ml of acetone was added into the flask and then 20ml of ethanol was poured into the homogeneous mixture. The resulting product was centrifuged (12,000rpm) for 5min and washed by ethanol for three times. Finally, the sediment was dried in the air to obtain carbon dots.

Before coating, GCE substrates were polished with finer grade aqueous alumina slurries (0.05μm after 0.3μm) on a polishing cloth, and ultrasonically washed in absolute ethanol and doubly distilled water for about 5min, separately. The clean surface of the electrode was then dried by nitrogen. To obtain GCG composite, 1mg of graphene and 1mg of carbon dots were dispersed in 1ml of doubly distilled water and ultrasonicated for 1h, forming homogeneous graphene and carbon dots suspension. Then 10μl of the suspension was coated on the GCE and dried under the infrared lamp to get graphene/carbon dots/graphene modified glassy carbon electrode (GCG/GCE). The sandwich-like PtCo-graphene/carbon dots/graphene composite modified glassy carbon electrodes (PtCo/GCG/GCE) were obtained by electro-deposition of PtCo nanoparticles on the GCG/GCE electrode in a coating bath containing 0.38mM H2PtCl6 and 20mM Co(NO3)2 using a three-electrode setup consisting GCG/GCE working electrode, Pt wire counter electrode and SCE electrode. The graphene modified glassy carbon electrodes (GN/GCE) were prepared by coating and drying of 10μl graphene water suspension (1mgml−1) on the GCE substrates. The electro-deposition of PtCo nanoparticles on GN/GCE were performed by a similar process as in PtCo/GCG/GCE to get PtCo/GN/GCE. The electro-deposition on each GCG/GCE, GN/GCE and bare GCE was performed using cyclic voltammetry (CV) scanning from −0.8V to 1.2V at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 for 20cycles.

Electrocatalytic performances of the synthesized catalysts were tested on a CHI 660C electrochemical workstation with Pt/GCE, PtCo/GN/GCE and PtCo/GCG/GCE as working electrode, separately. The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of the samples were determined by CV of the hydrogen adsorption /desorption within a potential range of −0.3 to 1.2V at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 for 6cycles in a 0.5M H2SO4 solution. The calculation of ECSA was based on the equation of ECSA=QH/(0.21mC·cm−2 ×mPt), in which QH (mC) represents the charge associated with hydrogen adsorption/desorption and mPt stands for the mass of Pt loaded on the electrode. To evaluate the electrocatalytic performance of the catalysts for methanol oxidation, CV measurements were carried out within a potential range of 0 to 1.2V at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 for 12cycles in the mixed aqueous solution containing 0.5M H2SO4 and 0.5M methanol. The chronoamperometry tests were conducted at 0.6V for 1h in a mixed aqueous solution containing 0.5M H2SO4 and 0.5M methanol. All solutions used in electrochemical tests were purged with N2 for 20min before measurements and saturated with N2 during the whole process of tests.

The carbon dots were characterized by TEM, fluorescence analysis and infrared spectrum analysis. TEM image (Fig. 1A) of the synthesized carbon dots shows that the average size is 3.2±0.6nm. When placed under a daylight lamp, the solution of carbon dots showed colour of light yellow (Fig. 1B insert a), however, when irradiated by UV light (350nm), the solution emitted green luminescence (Fig. 1B insert b). The IR spectrum (Fig. S1) indicates that the prepared carbon dots contain many oxygen functional groups (such as OH, COOH) which facilitate the consumption of intermediate species during methanol oxidation. Fig. 1B demonstrates the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots solution when excited under different excitation wavelength. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity reaches the highest value when the solution was excited at about 350nm and the fluorescence emission is at 424nm with no red shift, which reflects the diameter of the carbon dots is uniform.

The SEM images in Fig. 2 display the structure and morphology of the products. Fig. 2a and b indicate that PtCo/GCG catalyst exhibits a sandwich-like structure. The uniform dispersion of PtCo nanoparticles on graphene sheets is largely attributed to the introduction of carbon dots into the graphene support system, which can impede the aggregation of graphene sheets and thus provide abundant binding sites for PtCo nanoparticles to anchor. The carbon dots inserted between graphene layers cannot be observed, because the size is very small and may easily be covered by the background of graphene sheets. By contrast, the SEM images of PtCo/GN (Fig. 2c and d) reflect that the graphene sheets densely compact with each other due to the strong stacking force between different graphene layers. Thus, PtCo nanoparticles cannot disperse homogeneously on graphene support as indicate in Fig. 2c and d.

Moreover, the diameter of PtCo nanoparticles in PtCo/GCG catalyst is within the range of 9–25nm (as shown in Fig. S2a), which is much smaller than PtCo nanoparticles in PtCo/GN catalyst (62–98nm, Fig. S2b). The more uniform dispersion and much smaller diameter of PtCo nanoparticles anchoring on GCG than that of GN could be attributed to the decreased restacking of graphene layers and the three-dimensional sandwich-like structure of GCG with large surface area for PtCo nanoparticles loading on. The composition of PtCo/GCG catalyst was tested by EDS (Fig. S3), indicating the presence of C, O, Pt, Co elements and the atomic ratio of Pt/Co was about 1:3, which could further demonstrated that the PtCo nanoparticles were actually loaded on the GCG support.

The TEM image of PtCo/GCG catalyst (Fig. 2e) clearly reveals that graphene sheets and PtCo particles stack together orderly forming the sandwich-like PtCo/GCG catalyst. It can be conceived that graphene layers are separated by carbon dots forming sandwich-like support. The unique three-dimensional structure is beneficial to the uniform dispersion of PtCo nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 2e, PtCo nanoparticles uniformly anchor on the crumpled surface of graphene. It further demonstrates that the sandwich-like PtCo/GCG catalyst is successfully synthesized by observing the edge of the composite. Moreover, the sandwich-like hybrid catalysts could facilitate the diffusion of reaction species and further enhance the catalytic properties toward methanol oxidation.

The XRD patterns of PtCo/GCG and Pt/GCG are presented in Fig. 2f. For Pt/GCG catalyst, the diffraction peaks at 40.1°, 46.5°, 67.5° and 81.3° can be assigned to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) crystalline planes, which reflects Pt having the face-centered cubic (fcc) structure [31]. It proves that Pt nanoparticles with a good crystallinity have been successfully loaded on the graphene-based support. In the case of PtCo/GCG catalyst, the Pt diffraction peaks appear to have higher angles than that of Pt/GCG catalyst. This could be attributed to the contraction of lattice by introducing Co into the fcc structure of Pt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of electrochemical activity surface area (ECSA)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 depicts the CV curves of GCE coated with different catalysts in 0.5M H2SO4 solution within a potential range of −0.3 to 1.2V at a rate of 50mVs−1. The ECSA of the catalysts can be calculated by H2 adsorption/desorption area. It shows that H2 adsorption/desorption peaks for PtCo/GCG are wider and higher than that for PtCo/GN and Pt, which reflects that PtCo/GCG catalyst has more active sites. According to the equation of ECSA=QH/(0.21mC·cm−2 ×mPt), the ECSA value of PtCo/GCG is determined as 88.2m2 g−1 Pt, which is higher than that of PtCo/GN (81.3m2 g−1 Pt) and Pt (23.8m2 g−1 Pt). Combining with Fig. 2, this can further prove the uniform dispersion and small diameter of PtCo nanoparticles anchoring on GCG.

Fig. 4 displays the CV curves of methanol oxidation on PtCo/GCG/GCE, PtCo/GN/GCE, and Pt/GCE electrodes in the mixed aqueous solution consisting of 0.5M H2SO4 and 0.5M methanol within a potential range of 0 to 1.2V at a rate of 50mVs−1. The result shows that the onset potential of PtCo/GCG is lower than that of PtCo/GN or Pt, moreover, the mass activity at forward peak position for PtCo/GCG (437mAmg−1 Pt) is much higher than that for PtCo/GN (248mAmg−1 Pt) or Pt (93mAmg−1 Pt). These parameters prove that the catalytic activity of PtCo/GCG catalyst is better than that of PtCo/GN and Pt. The ratio of forward/backward peak values (If/Ib) in CV curves for methanol electrooxidation can reflect the anti-poisonous ability of catalysts. The values of If/Ib for PtCo/GCG, PtCo/GN and Pt are 1.57, 1.49 and 1.42, respectively, which demonstrate the PtCo/GCG catalyst having better anti-poisonous ability than that of PtCo/GN and Pt. The improved catalytic performance can be attributed to the embedding of carbon dots in-between the two-dimensional graphene sheets, which benefits the diffusion of reaction species, enhances electron conductivity between graphene planes and improves the dispersion of PtCo nanoparticles, and consequently enhances the synergistic effect of Pt and Co nanoparticles [32,33]. The functional groups (OH, COOH, etc.) on the surface of carbon dots are also beneficial to the consumption of intermediate species like CO [34].

As shown in Fig. 5 , the chronoamperometric curves of PtCo/GCG, PtCo/GN, and Pt catalysts have been measured in the mixed aqueous solution containing 0.5M H2SO4 and 0.5M methanol. At the initial stage, the current of these catalysts decrease rapidly owing to the generation of carbonaceous intermediates (like COads, CHOads) [23]. As for the current degradation rate, PtCo/GCG is lower than PtCo/GN and Pt, in the meanwhile, PtCo/GCG shows higher current density than that of PtCo/GN and Pt during the whole process. The above results present that PtCo/GCG has higher catalytic stability toward methanol oxidation than PtCo/GN and Pt. This could be attributed to the bi-functional mechanism [35,36] and the sandwich-like structure formed by introducing carbon dots.

In order to further assess the electrocatalytic properties of the synthesized catalysts, several related works have been listed in Table 1 for comparison. It can be clearly seen from the table that the value of ECSA for PtCo/GCG catalyst is higher than that of the other related catalysts. This demonstrates that the synthesized PtCo/GCG catalyst has large electrochemical active surface area which can provide more active sites for reactants. Moreover, the ratio of If/Ib for PtCo/GCG catalyst is relatively higher than most of other reported works, which means the catalyst we prepared having relative good anti-poisonous ability [37].

A sandwich-like PtCo-graphene/carbon dots/graphene (PtCo/GCG) electrocatalyst was synthesized by a convenient electrodeposition method. The sandwich-like structure with PtCo nanoparticles loading among the layers of graphene sheets was achieved, which was verified by using a range of methods (XRD, SEM and TEM measurements). The mean diameter of PtCo nanoparticles in PtCo/GCG electrocatalyst is much smaller than that of PtCo/GN. Compared with PtCo/GN and Pt electrocatalysts, the PtCo/GCG electrocatalyst exhibits higher electrocatalytic activity (with a typical peak current density of 437mAmg−1 Pt), better anti-poisoning ability (If/Ib =1.57) and stability toward methanol oxidation. The prepared composite has great potential for applications in DMFC.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405086, 21475071, 21601101), the Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province (No. ts201511027), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (BS2014NJ023, ZR2014BQ001), and Postdoctoral Foundation of Qingdao (2015113).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2017.08.027.
